paragraph: Researchers at the Valdrenheim Institute of Stellar Cartography announced on March 14, 2026, the confirmed detection of a super-Earth exoplanet orbiting the K-dwarf star HD 414892 at a distance of 38.7 light-years from the solar system. The planet, designated HD 414892-c, was identified via transit photometry using the Corvallis-9 Space Telescope Array, with a transit depth of 0.31% and an orbital period of 22.4 days. Radial velocity follow-up conducted over 18 months at the Nyberg Ground Station yielded a semi-amplitude of 4.1 meters per second, constraining the planet mass to 4.8 Earth masses with a 1-sigma uncertainty of 0.3 Earth masses. Atmospheric reconnaissance spectra collected at signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 87 revealed tentative absorption features consistent with water vapor and carbon dioxide at pressure levels between 0.1 and 1.0 bar. The host star exhibits a metallicity of [Fe/H] = +0.12 and an effective temperature of 4,820 Kelvin. Lead investigator Dr. Maren Solveig described the find as the closest known potentially habitable super-Earth within the southern galactic survey footprint, and the Valdrenheim team has applied for follow-up time on the Halverson-7 Array to characterize the secondary eclipse depth and constrain dayside thermal emission in the 4 to 12 micron range.

A fast radio burst catalogued as FRB-20261103A has been localized to a host galaxy at redshift z = 0.472, corresponding to a luminosity distance of approximately 2.74 gigaparsecs, according to a report released by the Thornwick Radio Transient Collaboration on April 2, 2026. The burst was captured by the Duvall Interferometric Synthesis Array during a 900-second integration window, exhibiting a dispersion measure of 842 parsecs per cubic centimeter and a pulse width of 1.3 milliseconds. A secondary microstructure component separated by 0.4 milliseconds was identified in the de-dispersed time series at a signal-to-noise ratio of 14.2. Polarimetric analysis revealed a rotation measure of minus 217 radians per square meter, suggesting the burst traversed a magnetically ordered medium consistent with a spiral arm environment in the host galaxy. No persistent radio counterpart was found at the burst position down to a 3-sigma flux density limit of 9.7 microjanskys at 1.4 gigahertz. The Thornwick team modeled the probability of a chance association between the burst and the host galaxy at less than 0.3%, using a Bayesian framework calibrated against 200 simulated random sightlines. The localization precision of 0.6 arcseconds is the tightest achieved for any FRB beyond redshift 0.4 using the Duvall Array.

The StarSeeker-IV mission team published updated photometric catalogs on February 28, 2026, containing 14,307 new candidate exoplanet transit signals identified in the mission's fourth data release, spanning observations from sector 61 through sector 88 of the spacecraft's continuous viewing zones. Among the candidates, 412 systems exhibit multi-planet architectures inferred from period ratios consistent with mean-motion resonance, including a five-planet chain around the G-type star SV4-221948 with period ratios of 1:2:4:8:14 and orbital periods ranging from 3.1 to 43.2 days. The median transit depth across the new catalog is 0.18%, with depths extending down to 0.02% for the smallest planet candidates around the quietest host stars. False-positive mitigation employed a gradient-boosted classifier trained on 3,200 labeled light curves, achieving a precision of 94.1% and recall of 88.7% on a held-out validation set of 640 objects. The catalog is available through the StarSeeker-IV archive portal maintained by the Feldhaus Space Science Center in Reykavik. Mission scientists estimate that roughly 2,100 of the 14,307 candidates will be confirmed as genuine planets within three years, based on follow-up allocation agreements with seven ground-based observatories, including the 8.4-meter Prism-South telescope in the Atacama highlands.

Cosmological measurements of the Hubble-Lemaitre constant obtained from strong gravitational lensing time delays have been released by the Quasar Lensing Consortium at the Drevnik Observatory Network, reporting a value of H0 = 73.8 plus or minus 1.6 kilometers per second per megaparsec based on a sample of 31 quadruply-imaged quasar systems. The analysis incorporated pixelated mass models for each lens galaxy, jointly constrained by Fermat potential differences, stellar kinematics from integral-field spectroscopy, and external convergence estimates derived from weighted galaxy number counts along each line of sight. Time delays were measured photometrically over baseline windows ranging from 4 to 11 years using the Ortenbach 2.1-meter Monitoring Telescope. The inferred H0 is in 3.7-sigma tension with predictions derived from the cosmic microwave background angular power spectrum assuming standard cold dark matter cosmology with a flat geometry. Consortium lead Dr. Agnieszka Wierzbicka stated that the internal systematic uncertainty budget was validated through six blind analyses conducted independently by subteams, and that lens modeling assumptions account for less than 0.8 kilometers per second per megaparsec of the total error. This result joins a growing body of late-universe measurements pointing toward H0 values in the range of 72 to 75, deepening what researchers are calling the canonical tension problem of concordance cosmology.

A collaborative team from the Novak Stellar Physics Laboratory and the Braunfeld Center for Nuclear Astrophysics has published the first empirical constraint on the neutron-capture branching ratio during the slow neutron capture process in asymptotic giant branch stars, using spectroscopic observations of 88 metal-poor halo stars with metallicities between [Fe/H] = minus 2.8 and minus 1.1. The survey was conducted with the Echidna High-Resolution Spectrograph mounted on the 6.2-meter Halberg Telescope, achieving resolving powers of 115,000 and signal-to-noise ratios above 200 per pixel in the 5,000 to 6,800 angstrom wavelength range. Measured europium-to-barium ratios in the sample span a dynamic range of 2.3 dex, with a subset of 14 stars exhibiting [Eu/Ba] greater than 0.8, indicative of r-process-dominated nucleosynthesis in their progenitor environments. The authors fit a three-component chemical evolution model comprising s-process yields from low-mass AGB stars, r-process yields from neutron star mergers, and a prompt r-process channel associated with collapsars. Best-fit model parameters imply that the collapsar channel contributes between 38% and 62% of total r-process barium at metallicities below [Fe/H] = minus 1.5, with a chi-squared per degree of freedom of 1.14 across 88 data points.

The Vorthex-3b exoplanet system has drawn intense scientific interest following the detection of a large, tidally heated moon candidate by the Pelrith Space Photometry Satellite, as reported in a paper submitted to the Astrophysical Communications Quarterly on May 3, 2026. Vorthex-3b itself is a cold gas giant of 2.1 Jupiter masses orbiting at 5.4 astronomical units from its host star, a solar-analog with an age of 6.9 billion years estimated from gyrochronology. The suspected moon, provisionally designated Vorthex-3b-I, was inferred from a series of transit timing variations with a semi-amplitude of 14.7 minutes, along with a 0.09% photometric anomaly preceding the primary transit by 38 minutes in 6 of 9 observed transits. Dynamical modeling of the timing variations yields a moon mass of 0.82 Earth masses and an orbital radius around the planet of 14.3 planetary radii, placing it well within the Hill sphere stability limit. Tidal dissipation estimates for a moon at this separation predict a surface heat flux of 0.38 watts per square meter, substantially exceeding the radiogenic baseline and suggesting a geologically active body analogous to those found in compact moon systems studied in theoretical planetary science literature. Independent confirmation attempts are scheduled using the Halverson-7 Array's photometric channel beginning in northern autumn 2026.

Observations of the galaxy cluster Abell-9471 conducted with the Trescott X-ray Imaging Spectrometer aboard the Luxor Space Observatory have revealed a giant bow shock structure at a projected distance of 1.3 megaparsecs from the cluster center, consistent with a merging subcluster traveling at a velocity of approximately 2,800 kilometers per second relative to the primary halo. The cluster is located at a redshift of z = 0.231, and the observed X-ray surface brightness discontinuity has a compression ratio of 3.1, yielding a Mach number of 2.7 assuming monoatomic gas with an adiabatic index of 5/3. Spectral temperature analysis across the shock front shows a jump from 6.1 to 11.4 keV, consistent with Rankine-Hugoniot shock conditions at that Mach number. Radio observations obtained with the Borgen Synthesis Array at 1.28 gigahertz reveal a diffuse radio relic co-spatial with the X-ray shock, spanning 1.1 megaparsecs and exhibiting a spectral index of minus 1.23 between 610 megahertz and 1.4 gigahertz, suggesting re-acceleration of fossil plasma by the merger shock. The integrated relic radio power at 1.4 gigahertz is 3.4 times 10 to the 25th watts per hertz. The Trescott team estimates a merger timescale of 0.4 billion years since first pericenter passage based on N-body hydrodynamic simulations of the system.

A new upper limit on the dark matter-proton spin-independent cross section has been established by the Crystalhorn Underground Detector, a 14-tonne liquid xenon instrument operating at the Meerow Deep Laboratory under 2,100 meters of water equivalent rock overburden. The result, announced on January 19, 2026, excludes cross sections above 2.3 times 10 to the minus 48 square centimeters at a WIMP mass of 40 gigaelectronvolts per square speed of light, a factor of 3.1 improvement over the previous leading limit at that mass. Data collected over a 412-day live-time exposure corresponding to 18.6 tonne-years were analyzed using a profile likelihood ratio test incorporating 97 observed events in the signal region against an expected background of 91.4 plus or minus 6.2 events from radiogenic neutrons, solar neutrinos, and surface muon-induced backgrounds. Electron recoil discrimination was achieved at a level of 99.5% using the ratio of prompt to total scintillation yield. The xenon isotope enrichment was 89.7% in Xe-129 and Xe-131 for spin-dependent analysis and natural abundance for spin-independent channels. Crystalhorn scientists noted that the result approaches the neutrino fog boundary predicted by the coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering floor at 30 to 50 gigaelectronvolts, and that the next detector phase, Crystalhorn-II at 40 tonnes, will require new statistical techniques to disentangle WIMP and solar neutrino signals.

Radio astronomers working with the Prandtl Aperture Synthesis Observatory have discovered a new class of rotating radio transient with a spin period of 76.4 seconds, the longest yet recorded among such objects, as described in a letter published on March 29, 2026. The source, designated RRAT J0441-27, was identified in a machine-learning-assisted search of 6,300 hours of archival survey data, with individual pulses reaching peak flux densities of 41 janskys at 154 megahertz. The duty cycle of 0.006% places it in a morphological category distinct from canonical pulsars, millisecond pulsars, and magnetars. A dispersion measure of 312 parsecs per cubic centimeter places the source at an estimated distance of 2.1 kiloparsecs using the Ostrikov-Chu electron density model, placing it in the Sagittarius arm at a galactic latitude of minus 4.3 degrees. Timing analysis over 14 months yields a period derivative of 3.8 times 10 to the minus 14 seconds per second, implying a characteristic age of 31,700 years and a surface dipole magnetic field of 1.7 times 10 to the 14 gauss. The high magnetic field and long period suggest the object may be an aged magnetar that has cooled below conventional X-ray detectability, a hypothesis supported by the absence of a counterpart in the Solexis X-ray All-Sky Monitor database down to a luminosity limit of 4 times 10 to the 28 ergs per second.

The Galactic Center Survey conducted by the Meridian Infrared Telescope Facility has produced a new stellar population catalog containing 2.14 million point sources within 200 parsecs of Sagittarius A-prime, the compact radio source at the dynamical center of the Milky Way analogue galaxy NGC 7041-A, located 18.4 megaparsecs distant in the Corvus-South group. Observations in the J, H, and Ks photometric bands achieved a 50% completeness limit of Ks = 21.4 magnitudes, enabling the first detection of the red giant branch tip at a distance modulus of 31.32 plus or minus 0.08 magnitudes. The nuclear star cluster surrounding the central compact object has a half-light radius of 4.2 parsecs and a total V-band luminosity of 7.1 times 10 to the 7 solar luminosities. Kinematic measurements of 4,800 stars using the Auriga Integral-Field Spectrograph yield a central velocity dispersion of 87 kilometers per second within the inner parsec, allowing a dynamical mass estimate for the central black hole of 6.3 times 10 to the 6 solar masses with a 15% statistical uncertainty. Comparison with predictions from the MBH-sigma relation established in nearby quiescent galaxies places this black hole squarely on the extrapolated scaling relation, providing a consistency check for central mass estimators at intermediate distances not accessible to direct resolved stellar dynamics from the ground.

Scientists at the Praxis Center for Exoplanetary Atmospheres have reported the detection of sulfur dioxide in the atmosphere of the hot Jupiter Pyrenax-7b, orbiting its F5 host star at a period of 3.14 days and a distance of 0.043 astronomical units, using transmission spectroscopy with the Halverson-7 Array's mid-infrared channel. The planet has a measured radius of 1.31 Jupiter radii and a mass of 0.93 Jupiter masses derived from radial velocity monitoring, yielding a mean density of 0.51 grams per cubic centimeter. A transit depth excess of 410 parts per million relative to the continuum was detected at 7.3 microns during three consecutive transits observed in January and February 2026, each at an air-mass-corrected signal-to-noise ratio above 32. Atmospheric retrieval modeling using a grid of 14,000 forward models explored temperatures between 1,100 and 1,800 Kelvin and C/O ratios from 0.1 to 1.4, favoring a chemical equilibrium solution with a dayside temperature of 1,470 Kelvin and a super-solar metallicity of 8 times solar. The presence of SO2 is interpreted as a photochemical product driven by UV irradiation of an atmosphere enriched in H2S and SO, consistent with accretion of sulfur-bearing planetesimals during system formation. The Praxis team notes this is the second claimed detection of SO2 in an exoplanet atmosphere among the 37 planets in their ongoing survey.

Gravitational wave detectors of the Seraphine Interferometer Network, a three-station array comprising instruments in western Australia, southern Greenland, and central Kazakhstan, have reported the detection of a compact binary coalescence event designated GW-20260218, with source parameters consistent with a neutron star-black hole merger at a luminosity distance of 410 megaparsecs. The event was recorded at a signal-to-noise ratio of 18.7 across the network, with an effective chirp mass of 4.1 solar masses and mass ratio constrained between 3.2 and 5.8 at 90% credibility. Sky localization from the three-station network yields a 90% credible area of 86 square degrees in the southern celestial hemisphere. An electromagnetic follow-up program was triggered within 28 seconds of the network alert, with the Colburn Ultraviolet Transient Explorer identifying a candidate optical counterpart at an AB magnitude of 21.3 in the r-band at 4.1 hours post-merger, decaying by 1.8 magnitudes over the subsequent 48 hours with a spectral energy distribution cooling from 6,000 to 3,100 Kelvin. If confirmed as a kilonova associated with GW-20260218, this would represent the most distant neutron star-black hole kilonova detected to date, enabling constraints on the opacity of heavy r-process elements synthesized during the merger at a distance more than twice that of the previous record holder.

The Cosmic Ray Extreme Energy Observatory, a hybrid surface and fluorescence detector array spanning 3,200 square kilometers in the Patagonian plateau, has published an updated measurement of the cosmic ray energy spectrum above 10 to the 18.5 electronvolts, based on data collected between 2019 and 2025 totaling 6.1 times 10 to the 5 events above threshold. The new spectrum confirms the presence of the GZK suppression feature with a spectral softening at 10 to the 19.6 electronvolts, measured at a statistical significance of 12.4 sigma above the extrapolated power-law index of minus 3.1 from lower energies. The all-particle spectrum above the ankle at 10 to the 18.5 electronvolts is well described by a broken power law with an index of minus 2.7 below and minus 3.4 above a break energy of 10 to the 19.2 electronvolts. Composition analysis using the depth-of-shower-maximum parameter Xmax measured in 1,800 air shower events above 10 to the 19 electronvolts shows a trend toward heavier nuclei with increasing energy, with the mean ln A rising from 1.9 at 10 to the 18.8 to 3.1 at 10 to the 19.5 electronvolts. This implies that proton-dominated sources are progressively depleted at the highest energies, consistent with a Peters cycle scenario. Anisotropy analysis of the arrival directions at a 12-degree angular scale reveals a dipole amplitude of 7.2% at a significance of 4.9 sigma, oriented toward a region of sky consistent with the centroid of nearby starburst galaxies within 25 megaparsecs.

A team led by Dr. Tobias Feuerbach at the Gronvik Institute of Radio Cosmology has published a power spectrum of the 21-centimeter hydrogen line intensity fluctuations at a mean redshift of z = 8.6, corresponding to an epoch when the universe was approximately 590 million years old, using data from the Meridian Drift Array's 512-element dipole configuration. The measurement spans wave numbers from 0.08 to 0.6 per megaparsec in comoving coordinates and reports a detection at the 3.8-sigma level in three independent k-bins, with a characteristic power amplitude of Delta-squared of 18.4 millikelvin squared at k = 0.15 per megaparsec. Thermal noise in the array was characterized through jackknife tests involving 212 independent 6-hour data segments, yielding a noise power consistent with the theoretical expectation to within 6%. Foreground removal applied a polynomial subtraction along the frequency axis in 1.8-megahertz sub-bands, achieving a foreground dynamic range of 10 to the 4.7. The measured 21-centimeter power amplitude is broadly consistent with reionization simulations in which roughly 30% of the intergalactic medium has been ionized by z = 8.6, constraining the ionizing photon budget of early galaxies to between 1.8 and 4.1 photons per baryon per unit redshift. The Gronvik team plans to extend the analysis to higher redshifts using the forthcoming expansion of the Meridian Drift Array to 2,048 elements, expected to come online in late 2027.

Astrometric observations of the binary star system Gliesner-402 conducted over a 14-year baseline with the Precision Astrometry Space Telescope have revealed a dark companion with an orbital period of 8.3 years and a semi-major axis of 3.9 astronomical units, according to a study released on April 11, 2026. The primary star is an M3 dwarf with a mass of 0.41 solar masses at a parallax distance of 9.73 parsecs. Orbital fitting of the astrometric residuals yields a companion mass of 4.1 Jupiter masses, placing it firmly in the planetary-mass regime below the deuterium-burning limit of 13 Jupiter masses. The derived orbital eccentricity is 0.23 plus or minus 0.04. The absence of any photometric or spectroscopic signature attributable to the companion implies a radius consistent with a cold substellar object, constraining the effective temperature below 550 Kelvin if thermal emission contributes no more than 3% of the total system flux in the Ks band. This would make the companion, designated Gliesner-402c given the presence of a previously known inner companion at 0.31 astronomical units, the coldest directly-inferred astrometric substellar companion around an M dwarf within 10 parsecs of the solar neighborhood. The Prism-South telescope has been allocated four nights in July 2026 to attempt a direct imaging detection in the L and M photometric bands.

An unusually luminous outburst from the X-ray transient source XTE J1846-219 has been recorded by the Solexis X-ray All-Sky Monitor and followed up with pointed observations from the Falconer X-ray Telescope, as reported by the Transient High-Energy Astronomy Group on February 7, 2026. The source reached a peak 2-to-10-keV unabsorbed flux of 3.8 times 10 to the minus 9 ergs per square centimeter per second on day 11 of the outburst, corresponding to a luminosity of 4.2 times 10 to the 38 ergs per second at an assumed distance of 6.8 kiloparsecs inferred from the hydrogen column density. Spectral analysis of the peak state revealed a thermal disk component with an inner disk temperature of 1.22 keV and a power-law hard tail with photon index 1.8, consistent with a soft-intermediate state. A Type-I X-ray burst was detected on day 19, lasting 18 seconds and exhibiting photospheric radius expansion with a peak blackbody luminosity consistent with the Eddington limit for a 1.4 solar mass neutron star, confirming the compact object identity and providing an independent distance estimate of 7.1 plus or minus 0.9 kiloparsecs. Quasi-periodic oscillations at 47 and 94 hertz were identified in the power density spectrum during the peak flux epoch, with fractional rms amplitudes of 6.1% and 3.4% respectively, suggesting Lense-Thirring precession of the inner accretion flow.

The Deepfield Spectrographic Survey released its third data release on March 3, 2026, containing spectroscopic redshifts and stellar mass estimates for 1.24 million galaxies spanning a redshift range of 0.05 to 5.8, observed over 2,800 square degrees of sky using the Mossvik Fiber Positioner System on the 4.8-meter Andvik Telescope. Galaxies were targeted using photometric redshift preselection from the Aurum Imaging Survey, with a redshift success rate of 91.3% for objects brighter than i-band AB magnitude 23.5. The survey data enable measurements of the galaxy stellar mass function across 11 redshift bins, finding that the characteristic mass M-star declines from 10 to the 11.2 solar masses at z = 0.1 to 10 to the 10.8 solar masses at z = 2.5, with a steepening faint-end slope from minus 1.35 to minus 1.62 over the same interval. Cross-correlation of galaxy positions with cosmic microwave background lensing maps from the Polaris-3 satellite yields a growth rate parameter f-sigma-8 = 0.421 plus or minus 0.038 at an effective redshift of 0.72, in mild 1.9-sigma tension with predictions from the baseline concordance cosmological model. The survey's two-point clustering measurements constrain the linear bias of luminous red galaxies to b = 2.14 plus or minus 0.07 at z = 0.6, consistent with a host halo mass of 6.8 times 10 to the 13 solar masses from abundance matching.

Infrared spectroscopy of a protoplanetary disk surrounding the young T Tauri star RO Ophiuchi-114, located at a distance of 142 parsecs in the Ophiuchus star-forming complex, has detected crystalline forsterite emission features at 10.3 and 11.3 microns, suggesting high-temperature grain processing in the inner disk, according to research published by the Dragonfly Disk Imaging Consortium on January 30, 2026. The star has a spectral type of K2 and an age of 1.7 million years as estimated from Hertzsprung-Russell diagram placement against Pinheiro pre-main-sequence evolutionary tracks. The disk has a dust continuum flux of 84 millijanskys at 1.3 millimeters, measured with the Alvarez Submillimeter Array at an angular resolution of 0.09 arcseconds, resolving the dust ring structure at radii of 28 and 56 astronomical units from the star. The forsterite-to-enstatite crystallinity fraction derived from spectral decomposition of the 10-micron silicate feature is 0.34, elevated relative to the interstellar medium baseline of 0.02 to 0.05 and consistent with thermal annealing at temperatures exceeding 800 Kelvin in the inner disk. A gap in the dust continuum emission at 38 astronomical units with a fractional depth of 72% is interpreted as evidence for an embedded protoplanet of 0.3 to 1.2 Jupiter masses, though the gap could also be attributed to a dust ring associated with a CO iceline at the local temperature of 21 Kelvin.

Measurements of the primordial helium abundance from metal-poor extragalactic H-II regions have been reported by the Nucleosynthesis Precision Group at the Veltheim Astronomical Society, based on optical spectroscopy of 74 low-metallicity dwarf irregular galaxies with oxygen abundances below 12 plus log(O/H) = 7.65. Observations were conducted using the Oryx Spectrograph on the 3.6-meter Feldhaus Telescope in the Canary Islands analogue site on La Palma-Nord island, achieving signal-to-noise ratios above 50 in the key helium emission lines at 4471, 5876, and 6678 angstroms. The analysis simultaneously fit helium line emissivities, electron temperature, electron density, ionization correction factors, and reddening using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampler with 10 to the 6 iterations. The inferred primordial helium mass fraction is Yp = 0.2463 plus or minus 0.0018, consistent at the 1.1-sigma level with the standard Big Bang nucleosynthesis prediction of 0.2470 for a baryon-to-photon ratio constrained by Polaris-3 satellite measurements of the cosmic microwave background power spectrum. This result provides an independent verification of standard nucleosynthesis at an epoch of roughly 180 seconds after the Big Bang, when temperatures fell below 0.1 megaelectronvolt and free neutron decay halted the synthesis of light nuclei. The Veltheim group plans to extend the sample to 200 H-II regions using the new Dragonstar Multi-Object Spectrograph.

The Orbital Resonance Survey collaboration has published an analysis of 48 compact multi-planet systems discovered by the StarSeeker-IV mission in which at least four consecutive planet pairs lie near first-order mean-motion resonances, identifying a population of tightly-packed inner planetary system architectures that underwent smooth convergent migration in their protoplanetary disks. The systems were identified from a parent sample of 6,400 multi-transit hosts by selecting those with adjacent period ratios within 5% of the 2:1, 3:2, or 4:3 commensurabilities. Transit timing variation analysis of 31 of the 48 systems yields libration amplitudes below 12 degrees for at least one resonant argument, confirming genuine resonant capture rather than near-resonant coincidence. A Bayesian population model fitted to the full sample of 48 systems constrains the disk migration rate parameter to a mean of 8.3 times 10 to the minus 5 astronomical units per year, consistent with Type-I migration driven by planet-disk torques at a disk surface density of 1,400 grams per square centimeter at 0.1 astronomical units. The most dynamically packed system in the sample, designated ORS-4814, contains six planets in a 1:2:4:8:16:24 resonant chain with orbital periods of 2.1, 4.2, 8.4, 16.8, 33.6, and 50.4 days and planet radii ranging from 1.4 to 2.8 Earth radii, making it the longest resonant chain currently confirmed among systems with six or more planets.

A new measurement of the optical depth to reionization has been derived from the large-angle polarization of the cosmic microwave background by the Polaris-3 satellite collaboration, yielding tau = 0.0541 plus or minus 0.0071 based on the TE and EE cross-power spectra at multipoles below 30. This measurement is based on the full six-year Polaris-3 dataset, representing a 23% reduction in uncertainty compared to the four-year result and a factor of 2.1 improvement over any ground-based measurement. The inferred optical depth corresponds to an instantaneous reionization redshift of z_reion = 7.68 plus or minus 0.79, consistent with a protracted reionization history extending from z = 10 to z = 6.5. When combined with external priors on the amplitude of scalar fluctuations from weak gravitational lensing surveys, the reionization optical depth measurement constrains the sum of neutrino masses to below 0.14 electronvolts at 95% confidence, assuming three degenerate neutrino species. This is the tightest cosmological neutrino mass bound from any single dataset without external spectroscopic galaxy survey information. The Polaris-3 team cautions that residual systematics from HWP polarization modulator synchrony constitute an additive uncertainty of 0.0015 in tau that is not included in the quoted statistical error, and encourages cross-validation against upcoming measurements from the Andean-CMB ground telescope network.

Optical interferometric imaging of the evolved red supergiant star Berevaar-Omicron using the Fringelock Baseline Array, a six-telescope optical interferometer with baselines up to 800 meters operating in southern Chile, has resolved the stellar photosphere at an angular diameter of 6.7 plus or minus 0.3 milli-arcseconds, corresponding to a physical radius of 1,430 solar radii at the Hipparcos-analogue parallax distance of 1.83 kiloparsecs. The surface brightness maps reconstructed from 14-hour interferometric observations at 700 nanometers show two prominent hotspot features with contrast ratios of 1.41 and 1.28 relative to the mean photospheric brightness, with angular extents of 1.1 and 0.9 milli-arcseconds. The hotspots are interpreted as convective cells comparable in scale to predictions from 3D radiation-hydrodynamic models of red supergiant convection, which predict cell diameters of 15 to 25% of the stellar radius. Multi-epoch observations spanning 180 days reveal systematic migration of the hotspot positions at angular velocities consistent with large-scale convective turnover timescales of 100 to 200 days. Simultaneous spectropolarimetric monitoring shows circular polarization signatures at the Halpha line at levels of 0.03%, consistent with localized magnetic field concentrations of 1 to 3 Gauss in the hotspot regions, providing the first interferometric evidence linking convective cell morphology to surface magnetic activity in a luminous red supergiant.

The Vega-Southern Hemisphere Deep Sky Survey has identified a population of 23 compact, high-surface-brightness objects at redshifts between z = 0.18 and z = 0.61 that exhibit morphologies consistent with strong gravitational lensing of background sources with Einstein radii between 1.3 and 3.8 arcseconds, as reported by the Klausberg Lens Factory team on April 25, 2026. The lenses were discovered through a convolutional neural network classifier trained on 180,000 synthetic lens images generated from a forward model incorporating the observed elliptical galaxy mass distribution and source galaxy morphology priors from the COSMOS field analog. The network achieved a purity of 91% and completeness of 84% on a held-out test set of 6,000 visually classified objects. Spectroscopic follow-up with the Mossvik Fiber System confirmed lens galaxy redshifts for 19 of the 23 systems and source redshifts for 11 systems, yielding Einstein mass estimates between 1.6 times 10 to the 11 and 5.4 times 10 to the 12 solar masses. The dark matter fraction within the Einstein radius ranges from 0.41 to 0.73, with a sample mean of 0.57 and a standard deviation of 0.09, consistent with the mass-dependent dark matter fraction scaling relation established from larger lens surveys at lower redshift. Three systems show evidence for substructure anomalies in the flux ratios of quadruply-imaged arcs, potentially indicating dark matter subhalo masses of 10 to the 8 to 10 to the 9 solar masses.

Researchers at the Novak-Feuerbach Center for Variable Star Research have reported the discovery of a new class of high-amplitude pulsating subdwarf B stars with pulsation periods between 4.8 and 9.6 hours and peak-to-peak photometric amplitudes exceeding 8% in the V-band, using data from the Corvallis-9 Space Telescope Array combined with ground-based spectroscopy from the Echidna High-Resolution Spectrograph. The four prototypical members of the class, designated sdBV-Mega pulsators and collectively known as the Novak-Feuerbach sequence, all reside in post-common-envelope binary systems with orbital periods between 0.9 and 1.4 days, white dwarf companions of 0.51 to 0.68 solar masses, and subdwarf B primary masses of 0.44 to 0.48 solar masses. Asteroseismic modeling using radial and low-degree non-radial p-mode eigenfrequencies computed from the Wentzel-Penrose stellar structure code identifies excitation by the kappa mechanism acting on the iron opacity bump at temperatures near 200,000 Kelvin in the subdwarf envelope. Echelle diagrams of the period spacing sequence reveal mixed modes with g-mode character in the 6 to 9 hour period range, providing sensitivity to the helium core boundary and constraining the helium shell burning mass to 0.003 plus or minus 0.001 solar masses. The binary interaction history of these objects suggests they represent a distinct formation channel from canonical sdBV pulsators via enhanced mass transfer driven by tidal resonance locking.

Mapping of the galactic magnetic field structure using dust polarization measurements at 353 gigahertz has been extended to a latitude coverage of plus or minus 70 degrees by the Polaris-3 satellite, revealing a coherent large-scale magnetic field component aligned with the galactic spiral arm geometry at low latitudes and transitioning to a striated pattern perpendicular to the galactic plane at latitudes above 40 degrees. The mean polarization fraction at high galactic latitudes is 7.4% in regions of low column density (NH below 10 to the 20 per square centimeter), with dispersion in polarization angle of 12 degrees. The power spectrum of polarization angle fluctuations follows a power law with slope minus 1.8 over angular scales from 0.5 to 10 degrees, consistent with magnetohydrodynamic turbulence in a medium where the Alfven Mach number is approximately 0.6. The ratio of turbulent to coherent magnetic field power is 1.4 on scales of 100 parsecs, estimated by decomposing the polarization map into E-mode and B-mode components using a spherical harmonic analysis. The E-to-B ratio in the thermal dust signal is 1.87 plus or minus 0.12, exceeding the value expected for purely isotropic turbulence and indicating a preferred alignment of density structures with the local magnetic field direction. These polarimetric measurements will serve as calibration templates for separation of primordial gravitational wave B-mode signatures from foreground dust emission in upcoming CMB polarization experiments including the Andean-CMB observatory.

The first direct imaging detection of the exoplanet Kesslerov-5d has been achieved by the Prism-South Coronagraph Instrument mounted on the 8.4-meter Prism-South telescope, as announced by the Falkenberg Direct Imaging Group on May 10, 2026. Kesslerov-5d is a 6.2 Jupiter-mass planet orbiting at a projected separation of 14.3 astronomical units from its host star, an F8 main-sequence star at a distance of 31 parsecs with an age of 280 million years estimated from chromospheric activity indicators. The planet was detected at a signal-to-noise ratio of 11.4 in the H-band after 4.5 hours of angular differential imaging at a field rotation of 72 degrees, with a contrast ratio of 8.7 times 10 to the minus 6 at the planet-star separation. Atmospheric spectrophotometry across the J, H, and K near-infrared bands reveals a spectral slope and methane absorption feature depth consistent with a T8 spectral type and an effective temperature of 1,050 plus or minus 80 Kelvin from a grid of Burrows-Feuerbach evolutionary model atmospheres. The surface gravity inferred from spectroscopic log-g fitting is 3.8 dex, yielding a dynamical mass of 5.9 plus or minus 1.1 Jupiter masses in agreement with the radial velocity mass constraint of 6.4 plus or minus 0.8 Jupiter masses from archival data. The planet's luminosity places it above the hot-start evolutionary track for its age and mass, tentatively suggesting a violent formation history involving gravitational instability rather than core accretion.

Spectroscopic measurements of the intergalactic medium Lyman-alpha opacity at redshifts between z = 5.4 and z = 6.2 have been carried out by the Quasar Absorption Line Team at the Gronvik Institute, using a sample of 84 high-redshift quasars with emission redshifts above z = 5.8 observed with the Asgard Spectrograph on the 10-meter Vestfjord Telescope. The effective optical depth of the Gunn-Peterson trough, measured in bins of 50 megaparsecs per hour in proper coordinates, shows a mean of tau_eff = 4.7 at z = 5.5 rising to tau_eff = 7.2 at z = 6.0, with a scatter of 1.9 dex that substantially exceeds predictions from models with a spatially uniform ultraviolet background. Eight lines of sight exhibit transmission spikes at z greater than 6.1 where the mean free path of ionizing photons drops below 20 megaparsecs, suggesting the presence of large ionized bubbles surrounding luminous galaxy overdensities. The cumulative distribution of opacity in 50-megaparsec windows at z = 5.8 is inconsistent with a uniform ionization model at the 4.3-sigma level, supporting a picture of late and inhomogeneous hydrogen reionization completing between z = 5.3 and z = 6.2. The team combined these quasar spectra with galaxy number density measurements from the Meridian Infrared Telescope Facility to show that lines of sight passing through galaxy overdensities have systematically lower Lyman-alpha opacity by a mean of 1.1 in log tau_eff at fixed redshift.

The Polarimetric Transient Survey Telescope has detected a new category of optical transient designated Type-IIx supernovae, characterized by broad hydrogen emission lines with a blueshifted absorption minimum at minus 24,000 kilometers per second and strong Halpha emission equivalent widths exceeding 400 angstroms at 20 days post-maximum light, in a sample of seven events identified in 3.1 years of operations. All seven events occurred in late-type spiral galaxies at redshifts between 0.02 and 0.09, with host-galaxy oxygen abundances spanning 8.4 to 8.9 on the Pettini-Pagel scale. Peak absolute magnitudes in the r-band range from minus 19.1 to minus 20.4, placing the class at the luminous end of Type-II supernovae. Light curve rise times measured from pre-explosion limits range from 16 to 24 days, and post-maximum decline rates of 0.02 to 0.04 magnitudes per day are slower than classical Type-IIP events. Radio observations of three events at 5 gigahertz reveal synchrotron emission declining as a power law with time index minus 1.3, consistent with interaction of the supernova shock with a dense circumstellar medium shell at 10 to the 15 centimeters from the progenitor. Ultraviolet spectroscopy of two events shows flash-ionization signatures of nitrogen and carbon in the earliest spectra, providing direct evidence for nitrogen-enriched circumstellar gas ejected in a pre-explosion mass-loss episode estimated at 0.3 to 0.9 solar masses per year in the decades preceding core collapse.

A new population of tidal stream stars associated with the disrupted dwarf galaxy Caerwyn-Nebula-4 has been identified in the Dragonfield Astrometric Catalog by researchers at the Braunfeld Galactic Dynamics Group, using proper motion and radial velocity data for 2.4 million stars in the solar neighborhood out to 7 kiloparsecs. The stream was detected using a modified frequency-angle wrapping technique in action-angle space, revealing 1,140 kinematically coherent stars tracing a great circle arc of 87 degrees on the sky at a mean distance of 4.1 kiloparsecs. Chemical tagging using NLTE abundances from the Asgard Spectrograph survey confirms that stream members have a mean metallicity of [Fe/H] = minus 1.6 with an alpha-element enhancement of [alpha/Fe] = 0.38, consistent with a dwarf galaxy origin and inconsistent with the Milky Way thin or thick disk distributions. Orbit integration in a triaxial galactic potential model yields an orbital period of 920 megayears and a pericentre distance of 6.2 kiloparsecs, suggesting the progenitor dwarf was tidally disrupted approximately 2 to 4 billion years ago based on stream length arguments. The inferred progenitor stellar mass of 3 times 10 to the 7 solar masses places Caerwyn-Nebula-4 among the intermediate-mass class of accreted satellite galaxies predicted by cosmological simulations of Milky Way-mass halo assembly histories.

Scientists using the Halverson-7 Array's near-infrared spectroscopy mode have obtained transmission spectra of the temperate sub-Neptune exoplanet Regalis-12b, covering a wavelength range of 0.6 to 5.3 microns across 11 transit observations accumulated between October 2025 and March 2026. Regalis-12b orbits an M2 dwarf star at an orbital period of 14.8 days and a separation of 0.076 astronomical units, placing it at the inner edge of the empirical habitable zone for that stellar type. The planet has a radius of 2.4 Earth radii and a mass of 8.1 Earth masses from radial velocity monitoring, yielding a bulk density of 3.4 grams per cubic centimeter. The transmission spectrum shows no significant molecular absorption features down to a 2-sigma limit of 8 parts per million in individual 0.05-micron bins, inconsistent with cloud-free hydrogen-dominated atmosphere models and inconsistent with pure water vapor models at a confidence of 97.3%. The featureless spectrum is best explained by either a high-mean-molecular-weight atmosphere dominated by CO2, N2, or water vapor with mixing ratios above 80%, or a high-altitude aerosol or condensate layer with a cloud-top pressure below 0.5 millibar. Bayesian model comparison favors the heavy-atmosphere scenario by a Bayes factor of 18 over the high-altitude aerosol model, given the observed planetary bulk density inconsistency with a pure steam atmosphere at 1-bar pressures. This result adds Regalis-12b to a growing sample of sub-Neptunes with inferred heavy atmospheres from space-based transmission spectroscopy.

The Falconer X-ray Telescope has discovered 14 new ultra-luminous X-ray sources in a deep mosaic survey of the nearby galaxy group Dorado-Borealis, located at a distance of 8.3 megaparsecs, based on 600-kilosecond total exposure observations conducted between August and December 2025. The newly identified sources have peak 0.3-to-10-keV luminosities ranging from 3.4 times 10 to the 39 to 1.8 times 10 to the 40 ergs per second, with the most luminous source, designated ULX-DB-03, showing variability by a factor of 6 over a 40-day baseline. Spectral fitting of ULX-DB-03 with a two-component absorbed disk plus power-law model yields an inner disk temperature of 0.24 keV, a hard photon index of 1.4, and an intrinsic hydrogen column of 4.3 times 10 to the 21 per square centimeter. If the peak luminosity represents super-Eddington accretion onto a stellar-mass black hole of 8 solar masses, the inferred geometric beaming factor is 12, consistent with the wind-dominated super-critical accretion regime. Alternatively, the luminosity is consistent with sub-Eddington accretion onto an intermediate-mass black hole of 150 solar masses. Optical counterpart identification using the Prism-South telescope in the V and I bands shows a blue point source at 3.7 sigma significance at the X-ray position, with an absolute V magnitude of minus 7.2 consistent with a luminous X-ray binary in a young stellar cluster.

Researchers from the Veltheim Observational Cosmology Unit have released the first measurement of the kinematic Sunyaev-Zeldovich signal from a sample of 4,800 galaxy clusters identified in the Aurum Imaging Survey, using CMB temperature maps from the Polaris-3 satellite at angular resolutions of 1.4 and 2.1 arcminutes. The kSZ signal is extracted by stacking temperature maps at cluster locations after filtering at angular scales corresponding to the cluster virial radius, using a pairwise momentum estimator calibrated against hydrodynamical simulations from the Veltheim Titan simulation suite. The mean pairwise kSZ amplitude at a comoving separation of 50 megaparsecs is minus 1.3 plus or minus 0.3 microkelvin, detected at a significance of 4.3 sigma. This measurement constrains the electron pressure profile outside the cluster virial radius, finding evidence for a shallower pressure profile beyond 2 virial radii than predicted by the standard Navarro-Frenk-White profile, consistent with the influence of baryonic feedback from AGN activity at radii exceeding 2 megaparsecs. The optical depth to electron scattering within the cluster sample is constrained to a mean value of 2.1 times 10 to the minus 3, in agreement with X-ray-derived electron density profiles from the Trescott instrument for a overlapping subsample of 420 clusters with both measurements. The kSZ amplitude is used to place a 95% confidence upper limit on the sum of neutrino masses of 0.19 electronvolts via its effect on cluster velocities at fixed halo mass.

Photometric monitoring of the active galactic nucleus in the Seyfert-1 galaxy Markarian-V771, located at a redshift of z = 0.034, over a seven-year baseline with the Ostrikov Photometric Monitor has revealed quasi-periodic oscillations in the optical continuum at a rest-frame period of 1,340 plus or minus 90 days, with a fractional amplitude of 18% in the V-band. The signal has been stable through 1.9 full cycles covered by the monitoring data, with a false alarm probability of 0.4% estimated from bootstrap resampling of the light curve. A competing period near 2,110 days is disfavored at 3.2 sigma based on Lomb-Scargle periodogram analysis and Bayesian information criterion comparison. The preferred interpretation is orbital motion of a secondary supermassive black hole in a sub-parsec binary with the primary, implying a secondary-to-primary mass ratio of approximately 0.1 given the primary mass of 4.7 times 10 to the 8 solar masses from reverberation mapping. The expected gravitational wave strain from such a binary at the Seraphine Interferometer Network frequency band is 8 times 10 to the minus 17, below current sensitivity thresholds by a factor of 400 but potentially detectable by the next-generation nanohertz gravitational wave background measurements from the Platypus Pulsar Timing Array within a decade. Optical reverberation mapping of the broad-line region continues with the Auriga Integral-Field Spectrograph to constrain the accretion state of the putative binary system.

The Meridian Infrared Telescope Facility has completed a census of brown dwarf companions to solar-type stars within 100 parsecs as part of the Companion Frequency at Intermediate Separations Survey, identifying 91 brown dwarf companions with projected separations between 5 and 500 astronomical units in a volume-limited sample of 2,200 FGK primary stars. The brown dwarf companion frequency in this separation range is 4.2 plus or minus 0.6%, confirming and refining the so-called brown dwarf desert, the paucity of brown dwarf companions relative to planetary and stellar companions at these separations. The companion mass ratio distribution shows a deficit at q = M_companion/M_primary between 0.01 and 0.05 relative to extrapolations from both the planetary and stellar companion distributions, suggesting distinct formation pathways for objects in this mass range. Mass estimates from H-band absolute magnitudes combined with the Wentzel-Penrose evolutionary models range from 14 to 78 Jupiter masses for the 91 companions, with a median mass of 41 Jupiter masses. Spectral type distribution peaks at L4 with a secondary peak at T2. System age estimates from chromospheric activity and gyrochronology of the primaries range from 0.4 to 8.1 gigayears, enabling an empirical calibration of the mass-luminosity relation for low-mass substellar objects at fixed age. The survey concludes that the desert floor is deepest at separations of 20 to 80 astronomical units and at primary masses between 0.7 and 1.1 solar masses.

Long-baseline pulsar timing observations using the Platypus Pulsar Timing Array, incorporating data from 67 millisecond pulsars timed over a 12-year baseline with the Prandtl Aperture Synthesis Observatory and supplementary stations in Japan and South Africa, have produced updated constraints on the nanohertz gravitational wave background spectrum. The latest analysis, submitted on April 4, 2026, finds a characteristic strain amplitude of h_c = 2.8 times 10 to the minus 15 at a reference frequency of 1 per year, with a spectral index of minus 0.66 plus or minus 0.09 in strain, consistent with a power-law background from inspiraling supermassive black hole binaries. A cross-correlation analysis of timing residuals between all pulsar pairs detects the Hellings-Downs angular correlation signature at a significance of 8.3 sigma, up from 5.1 sigma in the previous two-year analysis increment. The preferred model for the background is a population of circular supermassive black hole binaries with a mass function that peaks at 10 to the 9 solar masses and contributes most background power at orbital periods between 0.5 and 5 years. The measured background amplitude is consistent at the 1.8-sigma level with predictions from independent galaxy merger rate constraints and supermassive black hole mass scaling relations, suggesting no anomalous source of stochastic gravitational wave emission is required. The collaboration reports a 95% confidence upper limit on individual binary gravitational wave sources in the Virgo supercluster of h = 3.1 times 10 to the minus 14.

Astronomers at the Pelrith Space Photometry Satellite team have reported the detection of the first confirmed circumbinary planet in a system where both stellar components are pre-main-sequence, offering a unique window into planet formation timescales in young binary star environments. The binary system, designated PBS-2241, consists of two K-type stars with masses of 0.84 and 0.71 solar masses on a 14.6-day mutual orbit, located 310 parsecs away in the Lupus star-forming region at an age of 2.8 million years. The planet, PBS-2241b, has an orbital period of 98.3 days and a radius of 8.1 Earth radii inferred from the transit depth of 1.7%, placing it in the mini-Neptune to sub-Saturn size range. Transit timing of the primary and secondary eclipses reveals a nodal regression rate of 0.018 degrees per day, consistent with perturbations from the planet. Dynamical modeling constrains the planet's mass to below 0.6 Jupiter masses from the absence of measurable eclipse timing variations attributable to the planet's gravitational influence. The discovery implies that circumbinary planets can form or migrate to near the stability boundary within the first 3 million years of a stellar system's life, challenging models that predict circumbinary planet formation requires disk lifetimes exceeding 5 million years. The host binary disk was previously imaged at 870 microns by the Alvarez Submillimeter Array, showing a circumbinary dust ring at 35 astronomical units with a mass of 35 Earth masses in dust.

A comprehensive reanalysis of gamma-ray burst afterglow energetics using the Colburn Ultraviolet Transient Explorer and Falconer X-ray Telescope archives has been published by the Transient High-Energy Astronomy Group, covering 312 long gamma-ray bursts detected between 2018 and 2025 with measured spectroscopic redshifts. The sample spans redshifts from z = 0.08 to z = 5.1, with a median of z = 1.73, enabling the study of isotropic-equivalent energies from 10 to the 51 to 10 to the 54 ergs. Jet opening angle estimates from afterglow achromatic break times yield a beaming-corrected total energy distribution peaking at 10 to the 51.4 ergs, with a log-normal dispersion of 0.6 dex. The correlation between isotropic gamma-ray energy and peak spectral energy in the prompt emission, known as the Amati-Kervinen relation, is confirmed at a Spearman rank correlation coefficient of 0.77 with an intrinsic scatter of 0.31 dex in log Eiso. The afterglow X-ray luminosity at 11 hours post-burst correlates with isotropic kinetic energy at a slope consistent with unity, verifying standard afterglow blast-wave scaling and calibrating microphysical parameters: mean epsilon_e = 0.11 and epsilon_B = 0.003. When used as standardizable candles in the Amati-Kervinen plane, the 312 bursts yield a dark energy equation of state parameter of w = minus 1.07 plus or minus 0.14, consistent with a cosmological constant.

Molecular line emission mapping of the interstellar medium in the barred spiral galaxy Fornax-Annex-7, at a distance of 22.1 megaparsecs, has been conducted at 0.3-arcsecond angular resolution with the Alvarez Submillimeter Array, resolving individual giant molecular cloud complexes with linear scales as small as 32 parsecs. The survey covers CO(2-1), CO(3-2), HCN(4-3), and HCO+(4-3) transitions across the galaxy's central 4 kiloparsecs. The HCN-to-CO luminosity ratio, used as a tracer of the dense gas fraction, varies from 0.04 in interarm regions to 0.18 in the central starburst ring of radius 800 parsecs, where the star formation rate surface density reaches 14 solar masses per year per square kiloparsec. The molecular cloud virial parameter distribution has a median of 1.4 in the spiral arms and 0.7 in the central ring, suggesting clouds in the ring are more gravitationally bound and more efficiently converting gas to stars. A total molecular gas mass of 7.1 times 10 to the 9 solar masses is derived from CO(2-1) luminosity using an alpha_CO conversion factor of 4.3 solar masses per Kelvin per kilometer per second per square parsec appropriate for Milky Way-like metallicity conditions. The ratio of dense gas star formation efficiency, defined as the star formation rate divided by HCN luminosity, is elevated by a factor of 3.1 in the starburst ring relative to the disk, indicating that dense gas converts to stars more efficiently in the high-pressure environment of the nuclear ring.

The Feldhaus Space Science Center has released the results of a multi-wavelength variability campaign targeting 140 gamma-ray-bright blazars monitored simultaneously in optical, X-ray, and gamma-ray bands over a 3-year period from 2023 to 2025. The blazars span a redshift range of z = 0.03 to z = 3.12 and include 88 flat-spectrum radio quasars and 52 BL Lac objects. Cross-correlation analysis between gamma-ray flux measured by the Lumina-4 Space Gamma-ray Monitor and optical V-band flux from the Ostrikov Photometric Monitor yields a mean lag of minus 0.3 days (gamma-ray leading optical) for flat-spectrum radio quasars and a mean lag of plus 1.1 days (optical leading gamma-ray) for BL Lac objects, suggesting different emission zone geometries in the two blazar subclasses. Spectral variability in X-rays, characterized by the hard-to-soft photon index ratio, correlates with optical flux at a Pearson coefficient of 0.61 for BL Lac objects but shows no significant correlation for flat-spectrum radio quasars. The highest variability amplitude, quantified by the normalized excess variance, is observed in the 0.1-to-100-gigaelectronvolt energy range with a mean of 0.38 for the full sample. Two extreme variability events with flux doubling times below 6 minutes were recorded in BL Lac objects at z = 0.17 and z = 0.34, implying minimum Doppler factors of 28 and 31 from causality arguments and minimum bulk Lorentz factors of 14 and 15 respectively.

A survey of stellar tidal disruption events in low-luminosity active galactic nuclei using the Polarimetric Transient Survey Telescope has identified 18 events over a 4.5-year monitoring campaign in a sample of 28,000 galaxies with AGN luminosities below 10 to the 42 ergs per second, enabling the first measurement of the tidal disruption event rate per galaxy as a function of AGN bolometric luminosity. The inferred volumetric rate of 4.2 times 10 to the minus 5 events per galaxy per year in low-luminosity AGN is a factor of 2.8 higher than in quiescent galaxies, suggesting that the pre-existing accretion disk in low-level AGN enhances the radiative efficiency of fallback accretion. Peak luminosities of the 18 events in the g-band span a range from 10 to the 42.7 to 10 to the 44.1 ergs per second, with a mean peak delay from trigger to maximum of 24 days. Spectroscopic follow-up of 11 events with the Mossvik Fiber System reveals broad Halpha and HeII 4686 emission in all cases, with ionization ratios indicative of temperatures above 50,000 Kelvin. Black hole mass estimates from the host galaxy sigma-M correlation range from 3 times 10 to the 5 to 4 times 10 to the 7 solar masses, covering the poorly-explored intermediate-mass regime. The event rate and luminosity function are best fit by a disruption model in which main-sequence stars of 0.5 to 1.2 solar masses dominate the disrupted population, with a stellar pericenter impact parameter beta between 1.1 and 2.4.

Near-infrared astrometry of the Sculptor dwarf spheroidal galaxy conducted with the Precision Astrometry Space Telescope over a 6.8-year baseline has yielded proper motions for 11,400 individual red giant branch stars within the galaxy, enabling the first internal kinematic decomposition of the stellar populations in this classical Local Group satellite. The galaxy is located at a heliocentric distance of 84 kiloparsecs and has a central velocity dispersion of 10.3 kilometers per second from line-of-sight measurements. The proper motion dispersion profile in the plane of the sky is 16.3 plus or minus 1.1 microarcseconds per year at the center, declining to 8.7 microarcseconds per year at a projected radius of 1.8 kiloparsecs. Jeans modeling of the combined line-of-sight and proper motion velocity fields assuming a spherically symmetric stellar distribution yields a mass within the half-light radius of 4.8 times 10 to the 7 solar masses, consistent with independent constraints from line-of-sight kinematics alone. The inferred dark matter density profile shows a preference for a cored profile with a core radius of 0.7 kiloparsecs at a Bayesian evidence ratio of 8.3 to 1 over a cuspy NFW profile, contributing observational support to the core-cusp tension that arises in isolated dwarf galaxies between cosmological simulations and observations. The proper motion measurements also refine the Sculptor orbit, finding a pericentre of 67 kiloparsecs and an apocentre of 136 kiloparsecs, with the current position close to apocentre.

High-resolution spectroscopy of the carbon-enhanced metal-poor star Stensvik-3284 has revealed an extraordinary overabundance of the heaviest r-process elements, with [Pb/Fe] = 3.41 and [Th/Fe] = 2.87 at a metallicity of [Fe/H] = minus 2.71, making it the most extreme r-process-enhanced star known to the Novak-Feuerbach spectroscopic database. Observations were conducted over six nights with the Echidna High-Resolution Spectrograph at resolving power R = 120,000 and a mean signal-to-noise of 180 per resolution element near 4,000 angstroms. The star was identified in a photometric pre-selection of carbon-enhanced stars from the Vega-Southern Hemisphere Deep Sky Survey photometric catalog using CH-band absorption strength as a surrogate indicator. Abundance analysis via spectrum synthesis in local thermodynamic equilibrium for 41 atomic species spanning from lithium to uranium reveals a full actinide pattern consistent with a single r-process nucleosynthesis event, with a thorium-to-europium cosmochronometric age of 13.8 plus or minus 3.2 gigayears, the oldest individually-dated star in the Novak-Feuerbach database. Europium abundance [Eu/Fe] = 2.16 with [Eu/Ba] = 1.04 confirms overwhelmingly r-process-dominated heavy element enrichment, consistent with contamination by a single nearby neutron star merger event in the star's birth environment. This star provides a pristine nucleosynthetic record of the first r-process event in its natal cloud, offering constraints on neutron star merger rates within the first gigayear of galaxy evolution.

The Borgen Synthesis Array has detected a diffuse synchrotron emission halo surrounding the isolated elliptical galaxy Virgo-Strand-22, extending to a projected radius of 420 kiloparsecs at 144 megahertz, making it the most extended non-cluster radio halo associated with a single galaxy reported to date. The galaxy, at a distance of 48 megaparsecs, has a stellar mass of 3.2 times 10 to the 11 solar masses and shows no evidence for current AGN activity in radio, X-ray, or optical bands down to limits of 4 microjanskys at 1.4 gigahertz, 3 times 10 to the 40 ergs per second in the 0.5-to-7-keV band, and a 5007-angstrom [O III] line flux below 10 to the minus 17 ergs per second per square centimeter respectively. The radio spectral index of the halo steepens from minus 0.85 at 60 kiloparsecs to minus 1.82 at 380 kiloparsecs, indicative of synchrotron aging of relativistic electrons injected by a past AGN event estimated to have occurred 400 plus or minus 120 megayears ago. The total radio power at 144 megahertz is 2.1 times 10 to the 22 watts per hertz, and the minimum energy magnetic field strength inferred from the synchrotron spectrum is 0.41 microgauss. The spatial distribution of the halo is elongated along the host galaxy minor axis with an axial ratio of 2.3, suggesting lobe-driven jet inflation during the active phase. Particle transport modeling constrained by the spectral aging profile implies a diffusion coefficient of 3 times 10 to the 28 square centimeters per second in the intergalactic medium surrounding the galaxy.

The first spectroscopic detection of phosphine in the atmosphere of a temperate giant exoplanet has been reported by the Praxis Center for Exoplanetary Atmospheres, based on Halverson-7 Array mid-infrared transmission spectra of the warm Neptune Calyndra-9c orbiting an M4 dwarf with a period of 19.7 days at a distance of 0.091 astronomical units. The planet has a radius of 4.1 Earth radii and a mass of 19.3 Earth masses from radial velocity analysis, yielding a mean density of 1.7 grams per cubic centimeter consistent with a hydrogen-dominated envelope above a rocky core. Absorption at 4.3 and 10.5 microns was detected in three independent transit events at signal-to-noise ratios of 6.2, 4.8, and 7.1, with a combined significance of 9.3 sigma when optimally stacked. Atmospheric retrieval modeling incorporating equilibrium chemistry and disequilibrium photochemistry finds a phosphine mixing ratio of 4 plus or minus 2 parts per million by volume, elevated by a factor of 10 to the 3 above equilibrium chemistry predictions at the inferred atmospheric temperature of 420 Kelvin and a pressure of 0.1 bar. The disequilibrium phosphine abundance is interpreted as evidence for vigorous vertical mixing characterized by a diffusion coefficient Kzz of 10 to the 9 square centimeters per second, consistent with upwelling from a deeper, hotter region where phosphine is thermochemically stable. The discovery adds phosphine to the roster of detected disequilibrium species in exoplanet atmospheres alongside methane and sulfur dioxide.

A catalog of 6,200 new galaxy groups and clusters identified using the optical red-sequence technique in the Aurum Imaging Survey has been published by the Klausberg Lens Factory team, spanning a sky area of 5,200 square degrees and a richness range from 10 to 180 member galaxies within a projected radius of 1 megaparsec. Photometric redshifts for cluster members were estimated using a neural network trained on 280,000 spectroscopic redshifts from the Deepfield Spectrographic Survey, achieving a scatter of sigma_z/(1+z) = 0.017 and an outlier fraction of 1.3% for galaxies with i-band magnitudes brighter than 22. Richness-to-mass calibration was performed using 310 clusters with X-ray mass proxies from the Trescott instrument, yielding a power-law relation M500 proportional to richness to the power 1.16, with an intrinsic scatter of 0.22 dex. Cluster abundance counts in three redshift bins between z = 0.1 and z = 0.6 are compared to predictions from the Veltheim Titan cosmological simulation suite to constrain Omega_m = 0.302 plus or minus 0.011 and sigma_8 = 0.812 plus or minus 0.014, adopting fixed neutrino mass and spatial flatness. The S8 parameter sigma_8(Omega_m/0.3) to the power 0.5 is measured as 0.808 plus or minus 0.018, in 1.6-sigma tension with the Polaris-3 CMB value of 0.833 plus or minus 0.013, a mild discrepancy that the team attributes to potential systematic biases in the richness-to-mass relation at low redshift.

A 24-year baseline radial velocity survey of the solar-type star Velorum-482, conducted at the Nyberg Ground Station using the HARQ-3 spectrograph with a precision of 0.6 meters per second per epoch, has revealed a cold Jupiter analog at a semimajor axis of 5.2 astronomical units with an orbital period of 11.8 years, an eccentricity of 0.06, and a minimum mass of 1.14 Jupiter masses. The detection achieves a formal signal-to-noise of 31 in the periodogram peak. Interior to this outer companion, a known Neptune-mass planet at 0.41 astronomical units (period 93.4 days, minimum mass 14.7 Earth masses) was previously announced in 2016 based on 8 years of data. The extended baseline has now refined the Neptune's orbital period to 93.41 plus or minus 0.02 days and uncovered a long-period precession of its eccentricity vector attributable to secular perturbations from the cold Jupiter, with the eccentricity oscillating between 0.04 and 0.14 on a timescale of 6,400 years as derived from N-body integration. This secular architecture demonstrates that dynamically gentle cold Jupiters can accompany inner planetary systems without disrupting them, supporting the hypothesis that cold Jupiters with low eccentricities are preferentially associated with systems containing lower-mass inner planets. The occurrence rate of such companion architectures in the Nyberg survey sample of 420 FGK stars is 3.8 plus or minus 1.1%, consistent with predictions from planet population synthesis models employing Type-I and Type-II migration.

Infrared spectroscopy of 11 hot white dwarf stars in the temperature range 20,000 to 35,000 Kelvin using the Dragonstar Multi-Object Spectrograph has detected photospheric silicon and calcium at abundances of [Si/H] between minus 8.2 and minus 6.4 and [Ca/H] between minus 9.1 and minus 7.3, providing direct evidence for ongoing accretion of rocky planetary material. The white dwarfs were drawn from a magnitude-limited sample with Ks magnitudes brighter than 16.5 from the Meridian Infrared Telescope Facility, observed at resolving powers of 3,500. Infrared excess emission consistent with a warm dust disk was detected around 5 of the 11 stars at 3.5 and 4.5 microns, with fractional luminosities between 0.003 and 0.022. For three stars with both silicon and iron detections, the elemental ratios Si/Fe between 1.1 and 1.8 are consistent with chondritic composition of the accreted material, arguing against preferential volatile depletion of the parent bodies. The implied accretion rates derived from diffusion equilibrium modeling range from 4.2 times 10 to the 6 to 3.8 times 10 to the 8 grams per second, with the three dust-excess systems showing rates at the high end. Integration of the accretion rates over the inferred steady-state timescale of 10 to the 4 to 10 to the 5 years yields accreted masses of 3 times 10 to the 20 to 10 to the 22 grams per white dwarf, comparable to Ceres-sized or larger parent bodies and consistent with tidally disrupted members of a surviving planetesimal belt beyond the white dwarf Roche radius.

The Andean-CMB telescope network, a 42-element array of 6-meter dishes at an altitude of 5,100 meters in the high Atacama, has published its first season of power spectrum measurements covering multipoles from 200 to 8,000 in temperature and polarization, based on 1,400 hours of observation in a 700-square-degree field. The temperature power spectrum in the 3,000 to 8,000 multipole range resolves the fourth through eighth acoustic peaks, with peak positions at l = 1,024, 1,313, 1,597, 1,881, and 2,163 plus or minus 12, in agreement with concordance cosmological predictions to within 0.3%. The lensing power spectrum derived from the four-point function of the CMB temperature field is detected at a significance of 27 sigma with an amplitude A_lens = 0.97 plus or minus 0.04, consistent with general relativistic lensing predictions. B-mode polarization power in the 100 to 300 multipole range attributable to gravitational lensing is measured with an amplitude 6% below the theoretical prediction, a 1.8-sigma anomaly consistent with a universe with a summed neutrino mass of 0.09 electronvolts. The Andean-CMB network reports a 95% upper limit on the tensor-to-scalar ratio of r below 0.026, after joint delensing with the Polaris-3 lensing reconstruction, tightening constraints on inflationary models with a power-law potential. Systematics from atmospheric 1/f noise are controlled through azimuthal scan strategy and pair-differencing, achieving noise levels of 7 microkelvin-arcminutes in total intensity and 9 microkelvin-arcminutes in polarization.

A spectroscopic survey of supernovae remnants in the Large Magellanic Cloud Analog dwarf irregular galaxy LMC-prime, located at 78 kiloparsecs, has identified 34 candidate oxygen-rich supernova remnants using the [O III] 5007-angstrom line as a kinematic tracer, as reported by the Veltheim Stellar Autopsy Group on January 8, 2026. Observations were obtained with the Auriga Integral-Field Spectrograph on the 3.8-meter Odin Telescope, covering a field of 12 by 12 arcminutes at a spatial resolution of 0.4 arcseconds per element. Expansion velocities of the remnant shells were measured from [O III] emission line widths and spatial velocity gradients, ranging from 18 to 214 kilometers per second with a median of 67 kilometers per second, and ages estimated from the Sedov-Taylor deceleration model span 1,400 to 48,000 years. Twelve remnants show internal oxygen abundance variations with peak-to-trough [O III]/H-alpha ratios varying by more than 0.5 dex across the remnant face, indicating mixing of metal-enriched ejecta with swept-up ambient interstellar medium. The X-ray counterpart search using archival Falconer X-ray Telescope data finds thermal X-ray emission in 19 of the 34 remnants, with plasma temperatures between 0.18 and 1.8 keV. The spatial distribution of the 34 remnants traces recent star formation in LMC-prime with a correlation length of 180 parsecs and an overdensity near the brightest H-II complex by a factor of 3.4, consistent with clustered star formation driving correlated supernova activity in the host galaxy.

The Orbital Dynamics Consortium has completed a five-year orbital integration campaign for the known population of 182 exoplanets in compact multi-planet systems discovered by StarSeeker-IV with at least four transiting planets each, assessing the long-term stability of each system over 10 gigayear simulation timescales using a symplectic N-body integrator with a timestep of 1% of the innermost orbital period. Of the 182 systems, 91% remain stable over 10 gigayears when initialized at the best-fit orbital parameters, while 16 systems show orbit crossing or ejection events within 1 gigayear attributable to near-chaotic overlap of mean-motion resonances at high orbital eccentricities. The eccentricity distribution of the stable systems has a median of 0.022 and a 95th percentile of 0.089, confirming the dynamically cold nature of compact multi-transiting systems. Mutual inclination dispersion in the stable sample, inferred from transit duration ratios and transit impact parameter fitting, has a mean of 1.7 degrees and a standard deviation of 1.1 degrees, consistent with formation from a quiescent disk with moderate turbulent velocity. The 16 unstable systems contain at least one planet pair with an eccentricity exceeding 0.15 and a period ratio within 2% of a strong mean-motion resonance commensurability, suggesting that resonance-driven instability operates on sub-gigayear timescales in such configurations. These results provide empirical boundary conditions for planetary system habitability studies, as only systems remaining in secular stability for at least 4 gigayears are considered viable hosts for biological evolution on surface-habitable inner planets.
